'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市) is a historical concept in {{ill|Japanese history|ja|日本史}}, referring to the characteristics of {{ill|urban areas|ja|都市}} during the {{ill|medieval period of Japan|ja|中世}} (roughly 12th to 16th centuries). It describes clusters of urban settlements centered around religious institutions such as {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}.
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|warrior monks|ja|僧兵}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール}} free from external control.
== Notes == <references/>
== References == * {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏}}, ''Jisha Seiryoku no Chūsei'' (寺社勢力の中世), Chikuma Shobō, 2008. ISBN 978-4-480-06435-6.

It describes clusters of urban settlements centered around religious institutions such as {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}.
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|warrior monks|ja|僧兵}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール}} free from external control.

'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市) is a historical concept in {{ill|Japanese history|ja|日本史}}, referring to the characteristics of {{ill|urban areas|ja|都市}} during the {{ill|medieval period of Japan|ja|中世}} (roughly 12th to 16th centuries). It describes clusters of urban settlements centered around religious institutions such as {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}.
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|warrior monks|ja|僧兵}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール}} free from external control.
== Notes == <references/>

== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|warrior monks|ja|僧兵}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール}} free from external control.

'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市) is a historical concept in {{ill|Japanese history|ja|日本史}}, referring to the characteristics of {{ill|urban areas|ja|都市}} during the {{ill|medieval period of Japan|ja|中世}} (roughly 12th to 16th centuries). It describes clusters of urban settlements centered around religious institutions such as {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}.
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|warrior monks|ja|僧兵}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール}} free from external control.

In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|warrior monks|ja|僧兵}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール}} free from external control.
== Notes == <references/>
== References == * {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏}}, ''Jisha Seiryoku no Chūsei'' (寺社勢力の中世), Chikuma Shobō, 2008. ISBN 978-4-480-06435-6.

'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市) is a historical concept in {{ill|Japanese history|ja|日本史}}, referring to the characteristics of {{ill|urban areas|ja|都市}} during the {{ill|medieval period of Japan|ja|中世}} (roughly 12th to 16th centuries). It describes clusters of urban settlements centered around religious institutions such as {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}.
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|warrior monks|ja|僧兵}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール}} free from external control.
== Notes == <references/>

'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市) is a historical concept in {{ill|Japanese history|ja|日本史}}, referring to the characteristics of {{ill|urban areas|ja|都市}} during the {{ill|medieval period of Japan|ja|中世}} (roughly 12th to 16th centuries). It describes clusters of urban settlements centered around religious institutions such as {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}.
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|warrior monks|ja|僧兵}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール}} free from external control.
== Notes == <references/>
== References == * {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏}}, ''Jisha Seiryoku no Chūsei'' (寺社勢力の中世), Chikuma Shobō, 2008. ISBN 978-4-480-06435-6.

'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市) is a historical concept in {{ill|Japanese history|ja|日本史}}, referring to the characteristics of {{ill|urban areas|ja|都市}} during the {{ill|medieval period of Japan|ja|中世}} (roughly 12th to 16th centuries). It describes clusters of urban settlements centered around religious institutions such as {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}.
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|warrior monks|ja|僧兵}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール}} free from external control.
== Notes == <references/>
== References == * {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏}}, ''Jisha Seiryoku no Chūsei'' (寺社勢力の中世), Chikuma Shobō, 2008. ISBN 978-4-480-06435-6.
== See Also == * {{ill|Temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|ja|寺内町}} * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール}} * {{ill|Yamashina Hongan-ji|ja|山科本願寺}} / {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|ja|石山本願寺}} * {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} * {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}} / {{ill|Warrior monks|ja|僧兵}}

'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市) is a historical concept in {{ill|Japanese history|ja|日本史}}, referring to the characteristics of {{ill|urban areas|ja|都市}} during the {{ill|medieval period of Japan|ja|中世}} (roughly 12th to 16th centuries). It describes clusters of urban settlements centered around religious institutions such as {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}.
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|warrior monks|ja|僧兵}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール}} free from external control.
== Notes == <references/>
== References == * {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏}}, ''Jisha Seiryoku no Chūsei'' (寺社勢力の中世), Chikuma Shobō, 2008. ISBN 978-4-480-06435-6.
== See Also == * {{ill|Temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|ja|寺内町}} * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール}} * {{ill|Yamashina Hongan-ji|ja|山科本願寺}} / {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|ja|石山本願寺}} * {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} * {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}} / {{ill|Warrior monks|ja|僧兵}}

This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール}} free from external control.
== Notes == <references/>
== References == * {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏}}, ''Jisha Seiryoku no Chūsei'' (寺社勢力の中世), Chikuma Shobō, 2008. revision.text:
'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市) is a historical concept in {{ill|Japanese history|ja|日本史}}, referring to the characteristics of {{ill|urban areas|ja|都市}} during the {{ill|medieval period of Japan|ja|中世}} (roughly 12th to 16th centuries). It describes clusters of urban settlements centered around religious institutions such as {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}.
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|warrior monks|ja|僧兵}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール}} free from external control.
== Notes == <references/>
== References == * {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏}}, ''Jisha Seiryoku no Chūsei'' (寺社勢力の中世), Chikuma Shobō, 2008. ISBN 978-4-480-06435-6. == See Also == * {{ill|Temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|ja|寺内町}} * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール}} * {{ill|Yamashina Hongan-ji|ja|山科本願寺}} / {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|ja|石山本願寺}} * {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} * {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}} / {{ill|Warrior monks|ja|僧兵}}

For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|warrior monks|ja|僧兵}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール}} free from external control.
== Notes == <references/>
== References == * {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏}}, ''Jisha Seiryoku no Chūsei'' (寺社勢力の中世), Chikuma Shobō, 2008. ISBN 978-4-480-06435-6. == See Also == * {{ill|Temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|ja|寺内町}} * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール}} * {{ill|Yamashina Hongan-ji|ja|山科本願寺}} / {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|ja|石山本願寺}} * {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} * {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}} / {{ill|Warrior monks|ja|僧兵}}

revision.text:
'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市) is a historical concept in {{ill|Japanese history|ja|日本史}}, referring to the characteristics of {{ill|urban areas|ja|都市}} during the {{ill|medieval period of Japan|ja|中世}} (roughly 12th to 16th centuries). It describes clusters of urban settlements centered around religious institutions such as {{ill|temples|ja|寺院}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社}}.
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|warrior monks|ja|僧兵}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール}} free from external control.
== Notes == <references/>
== References == * {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏}}, ''Jisha Seiryoku no Chūsei'' (寺社勢力の中世), Chikuma Shobō, 2008. ISBN 978-4-480-06435-6. == See Also == * {{ill|Temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|ja|寺内町}} * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール}} * {{ill|Yamashina Hongan-ji|ja|山科本願寺}} / {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|ja|石山本願寺}} * {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} * {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人}} / {{ill|Warrior monks|ja|僧兵}}

'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市) is a historical concept in {{ill|Japanese history|ja|日本史|12=draft}}, referring to the characteristics of {{ill|urban areas|ja|都市|12=draft}} during the {{ill|medieval period of Japan|ja|中世|12=draft}} (roughly 12th to 16th centuries). It describes clusters of urban settlements centered around religious institutions such as {{ill|temples|ja|寺院|12=draft}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}}.
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|12=draft}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=draft}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力|12=draft}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷|12=draft}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家|12=draft}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府|12=draft}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深|12=draft}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺|12=draft}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|12=draft}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山|12=draft}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|warrior monks|ja|僧兵|12=draft}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|12=draft}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール|12=draft}} free from external control.
== Notes == <references/>
== References == * {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|12=draft}}, ''Jisha Seiryoku no Chūsei'' (寺社勢力の中世), Chikuma Shobō, 2008. ISBN 978-4-480-06435-6. == See Also == * {{ill|Temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力|12=draft}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|ja|寺内町|12=draft}} * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール|12=draft}} * {{ill|Yamashina Hongan-ji|ja|山科本願寺|12=draft}} / {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|ja|石山本願寺|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山|12=draft}} * {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|12=draft}} / {{ill|Warrior monks|ja|僧兵|12=draft}}
* {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|en|12=draft}} <!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--> * {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|en|12=draft}} <!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--> * {{ill|Sōhei|en}} <!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--> * {{ill|Shinto|en|12=draft}} <!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities-->
<!--Temple Precinct Cities--> <!--Temple Precinct Cities--> <!--Temple Precinct Cities--> <!--Temple Precinct Cities--> <!--Temple Precinct Cities--> <!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities-->

'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市) is a historical concept in {{ill|Japanese history|ja|日本史|12=draft}}, referring to the characteristics of {{ill|urban areas|ja|都市|12=draft}} during the {{ill|medieval period of Japan|ja|中世|12=draft}} (roughly 12th to 16th centuries). It describes clusters of urban settlements centered around religious institutions such as {{ill|temples|ja|寺院|12=draft}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}}.
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|12=draft}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=draft}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力|12=draft}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷|12=draft}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家|12=draft}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府|12=draft}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深|12=draft}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺|12=draft}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|12=draft}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山|12=draft}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|12=draft}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|warrior monks|ja|僧兵|12=draft}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|12=draft}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール|12=draft}} free from external control.
== Notes == <references/>
== References == * {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|12=draft}}, ''Jisha Seiryoku no Chūsei'' (寺社勢力の中世), Chikuma Shobō, 2008. ISBN 978-4-480-06435-6. == See Also == * {{ill|Temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力|12=draft}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|ja|寺内町|12=draft}} * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール|12=draft}} * {{ill|Yamashina Hongan-ji|ja|山科本願寺|12=draft}} / {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|ja|石山本願寺|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山|12=draft}} * {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|12=draft}} / {{ill|Warrior monks|ja|僧兵|12=draft}}
* {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|en|12=draft}} <!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--> * {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|en|12=draft}} <!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--> * {{ill|Sōhei|en}} <!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--> * {{ill|Shinto|en|12=draft}} <!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities-->

revision.text:
'''境内都市'''（けいだいとし）は、[[日本史]]上の歴史的概念で、[[中世]]（12～16世紀頃）における[[都市]]の特徴のひとつを示す。[[寺院]]・[[神社]]という宗教的施設を中心として成立した都市群を意味する。
「境内都市」は、日本史学者の[[伊藤正敏]]が指摘して成立しその後支持者が増えている概念だが、伊藤正敏本人は自著で「正直言って（この概念を指摘したのが）なぜ筆者が初めてだったのか理解できない<ref>伊藤(2008)、91ページ。</ref>」と述べている。また伊藤は「どうも語感が悪い<ref>伊藤(2008)、247ページ。</ref>」として今後別のネーミングに変更する可能性を留保している。
== 概要 == この概念が提示される前、寺社を起源とする集落は「[[門前町]]（参詣者などを対象とする商業的活動を行う都市）」と思われており、主たる機能は宗教的なものであり経済的機能はそれに従属するものと考えられていた。宗教は経済とは隔絶したものであるという現代におけるイメージもあり、門前町は大きな経済的機能を持つものであるとは考えられていなかった。
「境内都市」という概念は、そういう一般的な印象を見直し、寺社を起源とする大集落を政治的・文化的・産業的・軍事的な複合的機能を持つ都市として認識しなおそうという意図をもって提案されたものである。
「境内都市」は、もちろん個別に規模は異なるものの、大きなものは10万人以上の人口を持っていたものと考えられている<ref>伊藤(2008)、92ページ。</ref>。中世の日本にとって数万人という人口はまさに「大都市」と呼ぶべきものであり、「門前町」という言葉の語感による「小規模な集落」というイメージとは全く異なる。
== 境内都市の機能 == === 政治的機能 === 境内都市は、大寺院・大神社などの宗教的組織を基盤に置いて成立した都市であり、大きな寺社は日本の[[中世]]において[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]・武家（[[幕府]]）とならぶ「[[寺社勢力]]」という大きな政治的勢力となっていたことから、境内都市はそれらの寺社の政治権力の中心たる政治的都市という機能を有していた。
=== 文化的機能 === 境内都市は、大寺院・大神社などの宗教的組織を基盤に置いて成立した都市であり、大きな寺社は日本の中世において「学究の場」として大きな地位を占めていた。そのため境内都市は、文化的な機能を持つ都市という機能を有していた。この「学究の場」「文化的機能」は、単に宗教的な意味あいであるにとどまらず武術・医学・土木・農業などの実学をも含んでいたことから、境内都市は単なる政治的・宗教的都市であるだけではなく、経済的にも大きな存在として発展する原動力となった。
=== 経済的機能 === 境内都市は、そこに集中された学識に基づく実用的な学問と、宗教組織が要請する高度な工業的スキルを持つ職能集団に裏打ちされた、高度な工業的生産能力を持っていたことから、工業生産地としても高い地位を維持していた<ref>たとえば[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]の1360年に[[畠山義深]]が[[根来寺]]に対して盾や弓矢の納品催促をしていること、1584年に[[徳川家康]]が[[高野山]]に対して鉄砲500丁と僧兵の提供を申し入れていること、1585年に[[豊臣秀吉]]が寺社に対して武具鉄砲の製造禁止令を出していること、などから、有力寺社が軍需産業の一翼を担っていたことが強く推測されている。</ref>。
また、参詣者の往来も含めた人々の移動の核ともなっていたことから、交易地としても有力な存在であった場所が多いと考えられている。
=== 軍事的機能 === 境内都市は、多くの場合、軍事的機能をも併せ持っていた。経済力・軍需産業に基づく強大な軍事力を持っていただけではなく、寺院・神社はしばしば城郭として設計・施工されていた<ref>日本の中世における最初の[[山城]]は、1166年に建設された[[比叡山]]延暦寺であるという指摘がある。</ref>。戦闘員として[[僧兵]]や[[神人]]を多数抱えており、独自に軍事行動が可能であった。
この軍事力を背景として境内都市はしばしば域外権力の支配を受けない独立した[[アジール]]となっていた。
== 注釈 == <references/>
== 参考文献 == <!--この項目の執筆に際して参考にした文献。この項目で説明していることについてより深い理解を得るために役立つ文献は、ここには含めないで下さい。--> * [[伊藤正敏]]『寺社勢力の中世』ちくま書房〈ちくま新書〉、2008年。ISBN 978-4-480-06435-6。
== 関連項目 == * [[寺社勢力]] * [[寺内町]] * [[アジール]] * [[山科本願寺]]・[[石山本願寺]] * [[高野山]] * [[神人]]／[[僧兵]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:けいたいとし}} [[Category:日本の文化]] [[Category:日本の歴史]] [[Category:歴史上の都市]] [[Category:宗教都市|*]] [[Category:宗教施設]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

<!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities--><!--Temple Precinct Cities-->
==Merged second translation== {{ill|Temple complexes as urban areas|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市, *Keidai Toshi*) is a historical concept in [[Japanese history]], referring to a type of [[urban settlement]] characteristic of the [[medieval period]] (12th to 16th century). It denotes cities that developed around religious facilities such as [[temples]] and [[shrines]].
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his book, Ito noted, "To be honest, I don't understand why I was the first to point this out<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref>." He also stated that he might consider changing the term in the future due to its perceived awkwardness<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>.
== Overview ==
Before this concept was introduced, settlements originating from temples and shrines were typically regarded as {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}, or towns that engaged in commercial activities targeting worshippers. The prevailing view was that their primary function was religious, with economic functions considered secondary. Additionally, the modern notion that religion is separate from economics contributed to the perception that Monzen-machi lacked significant economic functions.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" was proposed to revise these common perceptions. It redefines large settlements originating from temples and shrines as cities with complex functions, including political, cultural, industrial, and military roles.
While the scale of these "Keidai Toshi" varied, some are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref>. In medieval Japan, a population of tens of thousands constituted a "metropolis," which contrasts sharply with the small-scale settlement image implied by the term Monzen-machi.
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban centers based on major religious organizations such as large temples and shrines. In medieval Japan, these religious institutions, alongside the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court (Japan)|qq=}} and warrior governments ({{ill|Bakufu|ja|幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|qq=}}), formed a major political force known as {{ill|Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Power|qq=}}. As a result, Keidai Toshi functioned as political centers for these religious powers.
=== Cultural Function === Keidai Toshi were also hubs of cultural activity, as major temples and shrines served as places of learning with significant status in medieval Japan. These cities provided not only religious education but also practical knowledge in fields such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural function drove their development as major economic centers, beyond their political and religious significance.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi maintained high levels of industrial production, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and by skilled occupational groups tied to religious organizations. For example, historical records indicate that prominent temples produced weapons and military supplies<ref>Examples include orders for shields and arrows at {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Negoro-ji|qq=}} in 1360, requests for 500 rifles and warrior monks by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ieyasu|qq=}} at {{ill|Mount Koya|ja|高野山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Koya|qq=}} in 1584, and Toyotomi Hideyoshi's prohibition of weapon manufacturing by temples in 1585.</ref>.
In addition to industrial production, Keidai Toshi also served as important trade hubs due to the movement of worshippers and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Many Keidai Toshi also had military functions. In addition to their economic power and production of military supplies, temples and shrines were often designed as fortifications<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be the {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|比叡山延暦寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku-ji|qq=}} on Mount Hiei, built in 1166.</ref>. They maintained significant military forces, including warrior monks ({{ill|Sohei|ja|僧兵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sohei|qq=}}) and shrine warriors ({{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}}), and were capable of conducting independent military operations.
This military power often allowed Keidai Toshi to function as independent {{ill|Asylums|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asylums|qq=}}, free from external authority.

revision.text:
'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市) is a historical concept in {{ill|Japanese history|ja|日本史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese history|qq=}}, referring to the characteristics of {{ill|urban areas|ja|都市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/urban areas|qq=}} during the {{ill|medieval period of Japan|ja|中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period of Japan|qq=}} (roughly 12th to 16th centuries). It describes clusters of urban settlements centered around religious institutions such as {{ill|temples|ja|寺院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temples|qq=}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines|qq=}}.
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple and shrine powers|qq=}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court (Japan)|qq=}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shogunate|qq=}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|qq=}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Negoro-ji|qq=}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ieyasu|qq=}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kōya|qq=}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|qq=}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|warrior monks|ja|僧兵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warrior monks|qq=}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/asylums (law)|qq=}} free from external control.
== Notes == <references/>
== References == * {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}}, ''Jisha Seiryoku no Chūsei'' (寺社勢力の中世), Chikuma Shobō, 2008. ISBN 978-4-480-06435-6. == See Also == * {{ill|Temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and shrine powers|qq=}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|ja|寺内町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaimachi|qq=}} * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Right of asylum|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamashina Hongan-ji|ja|山科本願寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Hongan-ji|qq=}} / {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|ja|石山本願寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama Hongan-ji|qq=}} * {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kōya|qq=}} * {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}} / {{ill|Warrior monks|ja|僧兵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warrior monks|qq=}}
* {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Precinct Cities|qq=}}
* {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaimachi|qq=}}
