==Merged second translation== {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市, *Keidai Toshi*) is a historical concept in [[Japanese history]], referring to a type of [[urban settlement]] characteristic of the [[medieval period]] (12th to 16th century). It denotes cities that developed around religious facilities such as [[temples]] and [[shrines]].
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his book, Ito noted, "To be honest, I don't understand why I was the first to point this out<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref>." He also stated that he might consider changing the term in the future due to its perceived awkwardness<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>.
== Overview ==
Before this concept was introduced, settlements originating from temples and shrines were typically regarded as {{ill|Monzen town|lt=Monzen town|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}, or towns that engaged in commercial activities targeting worshippers. The prevailing view was that their primary function was religious, with economic functions considered secondary. Additionally, the modern notion that religion is separate from economics contributed to the perception that Monzen-machi lacked significant economic functions.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" was proposed to revise these common perceptions. It redefines large settlements originating from temples and shrines as cities with complex functions, including political, cultural, industrial, and military roles.
While the scale of these "Keidai Toshi" varied, some are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref>. In medieval Japan, a population of tens of thousands constituted a "metropolis," which contrasts sharply with the small-scale settlement image implied by the term Monzen-machi.
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban centers based on major religious organizations such as large temples and shrines. In medieval Japan, these religious institutions, alongside the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court (Japan)|qq=}} and warrior governments ({{ill|Bakufu|ja|幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|qq=}}), formed a major political force known as {{ill|Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Power|qq=}}. As a result, Keidai Toshi functioned as political centers for these religious powers.
=== Cultural Function === Keidai Toshi were also hubs of cultural activity, as major temples and shrines served as places of learning with significant status in medieval Japan. These cities provided not only religious education but also practical knowledge in fields such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural function drove their development as major economic centers, beyond their political and religious significance.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi maintained high levels of industrial production, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and by skilled occupational groups tied to religious organizations. For example, historical records indicate that prominent temples produced weapons and military supplies<ref>Examples include orders for shields and arrows at {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Negoro-ji|qq=}} in 1360, requests for 500 rifles and warrior monks by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ieyasu|qq=}} at {{ill|Mount Koya|ja|高野山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Koya|qq=}} in 1584, and Toyotomi Hideyoshi's prohibition of weapon manufacturing by temples in 1585.</ref>.
In addition to industrial production, Keidai Toshi also served as important trade hubs due to the movement of worshippers and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Many Keidai Toshi also had military functions. In addition to their economic power and production of military supplies, temples and shrines were often designed as fortifications<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be the {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|比叡山延暦寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku-ji|qq=}} on Mount Hiei, built in 1166.</ref>. They maintained significant military forces, including warrior monks ({{ill|Sohei|ja|僧兵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sohei|qq=}}) and shrine warriors ({{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}}), and were capable of conducting independent military operations.
This military power often allowed Keidai Toshi to function as independent {{ill|Asylums|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asylums|qq=}}, free from external authority.
== Notes == <references/>
== References == * {{Cite book|author=伊藤正敏|title=寺社勢力の中世|publisher=ちくま書房|series=ちくま新書|year=2008|isbn=978-4-480-06435-6}} == See Also == * {{ill|Temple and Shrine revision.text:
'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市) is a historical concept in {{ill|Japanese history|ja|日本史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese history|qq=}}, referring to the characteristics of {{ill|urban areas|ja|都市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/urban areas|qq=}} during the {{ill|medieval period of Japan|ja|中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period of Japan|qq=}} (roughly 12th to 16th centuries). It describes clusters of urban settlements centered around religious institutions such as {{ill|temples|ja|寺院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temples|qq=}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines|qq=}}.
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen town|lt=Monzen town|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple and shrine powers|qq=}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court (Japan)|qq=}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shogunate|qq=}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|qq=}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Negoro-ji|qq=}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ieyasu|qq=}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kōya|qq=}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|qq=}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|warrior monks|ja|僧兵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warrior monks|qq=}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/asylums (law)|qq=}} free from external control.
== Notes == <references/>
== References == * {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}}, ''Jisha Seiryoku no Chūsei'' (寺社勢力の中世), Chikuma Shobō, 2008. ISBN 978-4-480-06435-6. == See Also == * {{ill|Temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and shrine powers|qq=}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|ja|寺内町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaimachi|qq=}} * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Right of asylum|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamashina Hongan-ji|ja|山科本願寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Hongan-ji|qq=}} / {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|ja|石山本願寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama Hongan-ji|qq=}} * {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kōya|qq=}} * {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}} / {{ill|Warrior monks|ja|僧兵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warrior monks|qq=}}
* {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Precinct Cities|qq=}}

==Merged second translation== {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市, *Keidai Toshi*) is a historical concept in [[Japanese history]], referring to a type of [[urban settlement]] characteristic of the [[medieval period]] (12th to 16th century). It denotes cities that developed around religious facilities such as [[temples]] and [[shrines]].
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his book, Ito noted, "To be honest, I don't understand why I was the first to point this out<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref>." He also stated that he might consider changing the term in the future due to its perceived awkwardness<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>.
== Overview ==
Before this concept was introduced, settlements originating from temples and shrines were typically regarded as {{ill|Monzen town|lt=Monzen town|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}, or towns that engaged in commercial activities targeting worshippers. The prevailing view was that their primary function was religious, with economic functions considered secondary. Additionally, the modern notion that religion is separate from economics contributed to the perception that Monzen-machi lacked significant economic functions.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" was proposed to revise these common perceptions. It redefines large settlements originating from temples and shrines as cities with complex functions, including political, cultural, industrial, and military roles.
While the scale of these "Keidai Toshi" varied, some are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref>. In medieval Japan, a population of tens of thousands constituted a "metropolis," which contrasts sharply with the small-scale settlement image implied by the term Monzen-machi.
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban centers based on major religious organizations such as large temples and shrines. In medieval Japan, these religious institutions, alongside the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court (Japan)|qq=}} and warrior governments ({{ill|Bakufu|ja|幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|qq=}}), formed a major political force known as {{ill|Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Power|qq=}}. As a result, Keidai Toshi functioned as political centers for these religious powers.
=== Cultural Function === Keidai Toshi were also hubs of cultural activity, as major temples and shrines served as places of learning with significant status in medieval Japan. These cities provided not only religious education but also practical knowledge in fields such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural function drove their development as major economic centers, beyond their political and religious significance.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi maintained high levels of industrial production, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and by skilled occupational groups tied to religious organizations. For example, historical records indicate that prominent temples produced weapons and military supplies<ref>Examples include orders for shields and arrows at {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Negoro-ji|qq=}} in 1360, requests for 500 rifles and warrior monks by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ieyasu|qq=}} at {{ill|Mount Koya|ja|高野山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Koya|qq=}} in 1584, and Toyotomi Hideyoshi's prohibition of weapon manufacturing by temples in 1585.</ref>.
In addition to industrial production, Keidai Toshi also served as important trade hubs due to the movement of worshippers and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Many Keidai Toshi also had military functions. In addition to their economic power and production of military supplies, temples and shrines were often designed as fortifications<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be the {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|比叡山延暦寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku-ji|qq=}} on Mount Hiei, built in 1166.</ref>. They maintained significant military forces, including warrior monks ({{ill|Sohei|ja|僧兵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sohei|qq=}}) and shrine warriors ({{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}}), and were capable of conducting independent military operations.
This military power often allowed Keidai Toshi to function as independent {{ill|Asylums|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asylums|qq=}}, free from external authority.

'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市) is a historical concept in {{ill|Japanese history|ja|日本史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese history|qq=}}, referring to the characteristics of {{ill|urban areas|ja|都市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/urban areas|qq=}} during the {{ill|medieval period of Japan|ja|中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period of Japan|qq=}} (roughly 12th to 16th centuries). It describes clusters of urban settlements centered around religious institutions such as {{ill|temples|ja|寺院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temples|qq=}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines|qq=}}.
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen town|lt=Monzen town|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple and shrine powers|qq=}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court (Japan)|qq=}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shogunate|qq=}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|qq=}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Negoro-ji|qq=}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ieyasu|qq=}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kōya|qq=}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|qq=}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|Sōhei|lt=Sōhei|lt=Warrior monks|ja|僧兵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warrior monks|qq=}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/asylums (law)|qq=}} free from external control.

==Merged second translation== {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市, *Keidai Toshi*) is a historical concept in [[Japanese history]], referring to a type of [[urban settlement]] characteristic of the [[medieval period]] (12th to 16th century). It denotes cities that developed around religious facilities such as [[temples]] and [[shrines]].
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his book, Ito noted, "To be honest, I don't understand why I was the first to point this out<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref>." He also stated that he might consider changing the term in the future due to its perceived awkwardness<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>.
== Overview ==
Before this concept was introduced, settlements originating from temples and shrines were typically regarded as {{ill|Monzen town|lt=Monzen town|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}, or towns that engaged in commercial activities targeting worshippers. The prevailing view was that their primary function was religious, with economic functions considered secondary. Additionally, the modern notion that religion is separate from economics contributed to the perception that Monzen-machi lacked significant economic functions.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" was proposed to revise these common perceptions. It redefines large settlements originating from temples and shrines as cities with complex functions, including political, cultural, industrial, and military roles.
While the scale of these "Keidai Toshi" varied, some are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref>. In medieval Japan, a population of tens of thousands constituted a "metropolis," which contrasts sharply with the small-scale settlement image implied by the term Monzen-machi.
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban centers based on major religious organizations such as large temples and shrines. In medieval Japan, these religious institutions, alongside the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court (Japan)|qq=}} and warrior governments ({{ill|Bakufu|ja|幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|qq=}}), formed a major political force known as {{ill|Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Power|qq=}}. As a result, Keidai Toshi functioned as political centers for these religious powers.
=== Cultural Function === Keidai Toshi were also hubs of cultural activity, as major temples and shrines served as places of learning with significant status in medieval Japan. These cities provided not only religious education but also practical knowledge in fields such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural function drove their development as major economic centers, beyond their political and religious significance.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi maintained high levels of industrial production, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and by skilled occupational groups tied to religious organizations. For example, historical records indicate that prominent temples produced weapons and military supplies<ref>Examples include orders for shields and arrows at {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Negoro-ji|qq=}} in 1360, requests for 500 rifles and warrior monks by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ieyasu|qq=}} at {{ill|Mount Koya|ja|高野山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Koya|qq=}} in 1584, and Toyotomi Hideyoshi's prohibition of weapon manufacturing by temples in 1585.</ref>.
In addition to industrial production, Keidai Toshi also served as important trade hubs due to the movement of worshippers and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Many Keidai Toshi also had military functions. In addition to their economic power and production of military supplies, temples and shrines were often designed as fortifications<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be the {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|比叡山延暦寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku-ji|qq=}} on Mount Hiei, built in 1166.</ref>. They maintained significant military forces, including warrior monks ({{ill|Sohei|ja|僧兵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sohei|qq=}}) and shrine warriors ({{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}}), and were capable of conducting independent military operations.
This military power often allowed Keidai Toshi to function as independent {{ill|Asylums|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asylums|qq=}}, free from external authority.

'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市) is a historical concept in {{ill|Japanese history|ja|日本史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese history|qq=}}, referring to the characteristics of {{ill|urban areas|ja|都市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/urban areas|qq=}} during the {{ill|medieval period of Japan|ja|中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period of Japan|qq=}} (roughly 12th to 16th centuries). It describes clusters of urban settlements centered around religious institutions such as {{ill|temples|ja|寺院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temples|qq=}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines|qq=}}.
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen town|lt=Monzen town|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple and shrine powers|qq=}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court (Japan)|qq=}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shogunate|qq=}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|qq=}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Negoro-ji|qq=}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ieyasu|qq=}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kōya|qq=}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|qq=}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|Sōhei|lt=Sōhei|lt=Warrior monks|ja|僧兵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warrior monks|qq=}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/asylums (law)|qq=}} free from external control.
== Notes == <references/>
== References == * {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}}, ''Jisha Seiryoku no Chūsei'' (寺社勢力の中世), Chikuma Shobō, 2008. ISBN 978-4-480-06435-6. == See Also == * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and shrine powers|qq=}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|ja|寺内町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaimachi|qq=}} * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Right of asylum|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamashina Hongan-ji|ja|山科本願寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Hongan-ji|qq=}} / {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|ja|石山本願寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama Hongan-ji|qq=}} * {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kōya|qq=}} * {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}} / {{ill|Sōhei|lt=Sōhei|lt=Warrior monks|ja|僧兵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warrior monks|qq=}}

==Merged second translation== {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市, *Keidai Toshi*) is a historical concept in [[Japanese history]], referring to a type of [[urban settlement]] characteristic of the [[medieval period]] (12th to 16th century). It denotes cities that developed around religious facilities such as [[temples]] and [[shrines]].
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his book, Ito noted, "To be honest, I don't understand why I was the first to point this out<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref>." He also stated that he might consider changing the term in the future due to its perceived awkwardness<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>.
== Overview ==
Before this concept was introduced, settlements originating from temples and shrines were typically regarded as {{ill|Monzen town|lt=Monzen town|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}, or towns that engaged in commercial activities targeting worshippers. The prevailing view was that their primary function was religious, with economic functions considered secondary. Additionally, the modern notion that religion is separate from economics contributed to the perception that Monzen-machi lacked significant economic functions.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" was proposed to revise these common perceptions. It redefines large settlements originating from temples and shrines as cities with complex functions, including political, cultural, industrial, and military roles.
While the scale of these "Keidai Toshi" varied, some are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref>. In medieval Japan, a population of tens of thousands constituted a "metropolis," which contrasts sharply with the small-scale settlement image implied by the term Monzen-machi.
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban centers based on major religious organizations such as large temples and shrines. In medieval Japan, these religious institutions, alongside the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court (Japan)|qq=}} and warrior governments ({{ill|Bakufu|ja|幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|qq=}}), formed a major political force known as {{ill|Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Power|qq=}}. As a result, Keidai Toshi functioned as political centers for these religious powers.
=== Cultural Function === Keidai Toshi were also hubs of cultural activity, as major temples and shrines served as places of learning with significant status in medieval Japan. These cities provided not only religious education but also practical knowledge in fields such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural function drove their development as major economic centers, beyond their political and religious significance.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi maintained high levels of industrial production, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and by skilled occupational groups tied to religious organizations. For example, historical records indicate that prominent temples produced weapons and military supplies<ref>Examples include orders for shields and arrows at {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Negoro-ji|qq=}} in 1360, requests for 500 rifles and warrior monks by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ieyasu|qq=}} at {{ill|Mount Koya|ja|高野山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Koya|qq=}} in 1584, and Toyotomi Hideyoshi's prohibition of weapon manufacturing by temples in 1585.</ref>.
In addition to industrial production, Keidai Toshi also served as important trade hubs due to the movement of worshippers and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Many Keidai Toshi also had military functions. In addition to their economic power and production of military supplies, temples and shrines were often designed as fortifications<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be the {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|比叡山延暦寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku-ji|qq=}} on Mount Hiei, built in 1166.</ref>. They maintained significant military forces, including warrior monks ({{ill|Sohei|ja|僧兵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sohei|qq=}}) and shrine warriors ({{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}}), and were capable of conducting independent military operations.
This military power often allowed Keidai Toshi to function as independent {{ill|Asylums|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asylums|qq=}}, free from external authority.

revision.text:
'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市) is a historical concept in {{ill|Japanese history|ja|日本史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese history|qq=}}, referring to the characteristics of {{ill|urban areas|ja|都市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/urban areas|qq=}} during the {{ill|medieval period of Japan|ja|中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period of Japan|qq=}} (roughly 12th to 16th centuries). It describes clusters of urban settlements centered around religious institutions such as {{ill|temples|ja|寺院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temples|qq=}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines|qq=}}.
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen town|lt=Monzen town|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple and shrine powers|qq=}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court (Japan)|qq=}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shogunate|qq=}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|qq=}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Negoro-ji|qq=}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ieyasu|qq=}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kōya|qq=}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|qq=}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|Sōhei|lt=Sōhei|lt=Warrior monks|ja|僧兵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warrior monks|qq=}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/asylums (law)|qq=}} free from external control.
== Notes == <references/>
== References == * {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}}, ''Jisha Seiryoku no Chūsei'' (寺社勢力の中世), Chikuma Shobō, 2008. ISBN 978-4-480-06435-6. == See Also == * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and shrine powers|qq=}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|ja|寺内町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaimachi|qq=}} * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Right of asylum|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamashina Hongan-ji|ja|山科本願寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashina Hongan-ji|qq=}} / {{ill|Ishiyama Hongan-ji|ja|石山本願寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama Hongan-ji|qq=}} * {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kōya|qq=}} * {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}} / {{ill|Sōhei|lt=Sōhei|lt=Warrior monks|ja|僧兵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warrior monks|qq=}}

==Merged second translation== {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市, *Keidai Toshi*) is a historical concept in [[Japanese history]], referring to a type of [[urban settlement]] characteristic of the [[medieval period]] (12th to 16th century). It denotes cities that developed around religious facilities such as [[temples]] and [[shrines]].
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his book, Ito noted, "To be honest, I don't understand why I was the first to point this out<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref>." He also stated that he might consider changing the term in the future due to its perceived awkwardness<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>.
== Overview ==
Before this concept was introduced, settlements originating from temples and shrines were typically regarded as {{ill|Monzen town|lt=Monzen town|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}, or towns that engaged in commercial activities targeting worshippers. The prevailing view was that their primary function was religious, with economic functions considered secondary. Additionally, the modern notion that religion is separate from economics contributed to the perception that Monzen-machi lacked significant economic functions.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" was proposed to revise these common perceptions. It redefines large settlements originating from temples and shrines as cities with complex functions, including political, cultural, industrial, and military roles.
While the scale of these "Keidai Toshi" varied, some are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref>. In medieval Japan, a population of tens of thousands constituted a "metropolis," which contrasts sharply with the small-scale settlement image implied by the term Monzen-machi.
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban centers based on major religious organizations such as large temples and shrines. In medieval Japan, these religious institutions, alongside the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court (Japan)|qq=}} and warrior governments ({{ill|Bakufu|ja|幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|qq=}}), formed a major political force known as {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Power|qq=}}. As a result, Keidai Toshi functioned as political centers for these religious powers.
=== Cultural Function === Keidai Toshi were also hubs of cultural activity, as major temples and shrines served as places of learning with significant status in medieval Japan. These cities provided not only religious education but also practical knowledge in fields such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural function drove their development as major economic centers, beyond their political and religious significance.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi maintained high levels of industrial production, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and by skilled occupational groups tied to religious organizations. For example, historical records indicate that prominent temples produced weapons and military supplies<ref>Examples include orders for shields and arrows at {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Negoro-ji|qq=}} in 1360, requests for 500 rifles and warrior monks by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ieyasu|qq=}} at {{ill|Mount Koya|ja|高野山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Koya|qq=}} in 1584, and Toyotomi Hideyoshi's prohibition of weapon manufacturing by temples in 1585.</ref>.
In addition to industrial production, Keidai Toshi also served as important trade hubs due to the movement of worshippers and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Many Keidai Toshi also had military functions. In addition to their economic power and production of military supplies, temples and shrines were often designed as fortifications<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be the {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|比叡山延暦寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku-ji|qq=}} on Mount Hiei, built in 1166.</ref>. They maintained significant military forces, including warrior monks ({{ill|Sohei|ja|僧兵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sohei|qq=}}) and shrine warriors ({{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}}), and were capable of conducting independent military operations.
This military power often allowed Keidai Toshi to function as independent {{ill|Asylums|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asylums|qq=}}, free from external authority.

'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市) is a historical concept in {{ill|Japanese history|ja|日本史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese history|qq=}}, referring to the characteristics of {{ill|urban areas|ja|都市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/urban areas|qq=}} during the {{ill|medieval period of Japan|ja|中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period of Japan|qq=}} (roughly 12th to 16th centuries). It describes clusters of urban settlements centered around religious institutions such as {{ill|temples|ja|寺院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temples|qq=}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines|qq=}}.
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen town|lt=Monzen town|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple and shrine powers|qq=}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court (Japan)|qq=}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shogunate|qq=}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|qq=}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Negoro-ji|qq=}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ieyasu|qq=}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kōya|qq=}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|qq=}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|Sōhei|lt=Sōhei|lt=Warrior monks|ja|僧兵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warrior monks|qq=}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/asylums (law)|qq=}} free from external control.

==Merged second translation== {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市, *Keidai Toshi*) is a historical concept in [[Japanese history]], referring to a type of [[urban settlement]] characteristic of the [[medieval period]] (12th to 16th century). It denotes cities that developed around religious facilities such as [[temples]] and [[shrines]].
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his book, Ito noted, "To be honest, I don't understand why I was the first to point this out<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref>." He also stated that he might consider changing the term in the future due to its perceived awkwardness<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>.
== Overview ==
Before this concept was introduced, settlements originating from temples and shrines were typically regarded as {{ill|Monzen town|lt=Monzen town|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}, or towns that engaged in commercial activities targeting worshippers. The prevailing view was that their primary function was religious, with economic functions considered secondary. Additionally, the modern notion that religion is separate from economics contributed to the perception that Monzen-machi lacked significant economic functions.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" was proposed to revise these common perceptions. It redefines large settlements originating from temples and shrines as cities with complex functions, including political, cultural, industrial, and military roles.
While the scale of these "Keidai Toshi" varied, some are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref>. In medieval Japan, a population of tens of thousands constituted a "metropolis," which contrasts sharply with the small-scale settlement image implied by the term Monzen-machi.
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban centers based on major religious organizations such as large temples and shrines. In medieval Japan, these religious institutions, alongside the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court (Japan)|qq=}} and warrior governments ({{ill|Bakufu|ja|幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|qq=}}), formed a major political force known as {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Power|qq=}}. As a result, Keidai Toshi functioned as political centers for these religious powers.
=== Cultural Function === Keidai Toshi were also hubs of cultural activity, as major temples and shrines served as places of learning with significant status in medieval Japan. These cities provided not only religious education but also practical knowledge in fields such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural function drove their development as major economic centers, beyond their political and religious significance.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi maintained high levels of industrial production, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and by skilled occupational groups tied to religious organizations. For example, historical records indicate that prominent temples produced weapons and military supplies<ref>Examples include orders for shields and arrows at {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Negoro-ji|qq=}} in 1360, requests for 500 rifles and warrior monks by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ieyasu|qq=}} at {{ill|Mount Koya|ja|高野山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Koya|qq=}} in 1584, and Toyotomi Hideyoshi's prohibition of weapon manufacturing by temples in 1585.</ref>.
In addition to industrial production, Keidai Toshi also served as important trade hubs due to the movement of worshippers and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Many Keidai Toshi also had military functions. In addition to their economic power and production of military supplies, temples and shrines were often designed as fortifications<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be the {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|比叡山延暦寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku-ji|qq=}} on Mount Hiei, built in 1166.</ref>. They maintained significant military forces, including warrior monks ({{ill|Sohei|ja|僧兵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sohei|qq=}}) and shrine warriors ({{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}}), and were capable of conducting independent military operations.
This military power often allowed Keidai Toshi to function as independent {{ill|Asylums|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asylums|qq=}}, free from external authority.

'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市) is a historical concept in {{ill|Japanese history|ja|日本史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese history|qq=}}, referring to the characteristics of {{ill|urban areas|ja|都市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/urban areas|qq=}} during the {{ill|medieval period of Japan|ja|中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period of Japan|qq=}} (roughly 12th to 16th centuries). It describes clusters of urban settlements centered around religious institutions such as {{ill|temples|ja|寺院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temples|qq=}} and {{ill|shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines|qq=}}.
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen town|lt=Monzen town|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple and shrine powers|qq=}}, rivaling the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court (Japan)|qq=}} and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shogunate|qq=}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|qq=}} urged {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Negoro-ji|qq=}} to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ieyasu|qq=}} requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kōya|qq=}}.<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hiei|qq=}}, built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of {{ill|Sōhei|lt=Sōhei|lt=Warrior monks|ja|僧兵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warrior monks|qq=}} and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/asylums (law)|qq=}} free from external control.

==Merged second translation== {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple complexes as urban areas|qq=}}, functioned as proto-megalopolises.
'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市, *Keidai Toshi*) is a historical concept in [[Japanese history]], referring to a type of [[urban settlement]] characteristic of the [[medieval period]] (12th to 16th century). It denotes cities that developed around religious facilities such as [[temples]] and [[shrines]].
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his book, Ito noted, "To be honest, I don't understand why I was the first to point this out<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref>." He also stated that he might consider changing the term in the future due to its perceived awkwardness<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>.
== Overview ==
Before this concept was introduced, settlements originating from temples and shrines were typically regarded as {{ill|Monzen town|lt=Monzen town|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}, or towns that engaged in commercial activities targeting worshippers. The prevailing view was that their primary function was religious, with economic functions considered secondary. Additionally, the modern notion that religion is separate from economics contributed to the perception that Monzen-machi lacked significant economic functions.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" was proposed to revise these common perceptions. It redefines large settlements originating from temples and shrines as cities with complex functions, including political, cultural, industrial, and military roles.
While the scale of these "Keidai Toshi" varied, some are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref>. In medieval Japan, a population of tens of thousands constituted a "metropolis," which contrasts sharply with the small-scale settlement image implied by the term Monzen-machi.
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban centers based on major religious organizations such as large temples and shrines. In medieval Japan, these religious institutions, alongside the {{ill|Imperial Court (Japan)|ja|朝廷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court (Japan)|qq=}} and warrior governments ({{ill|Bakufu|ja|幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakufu|qq=}}), formed a major political force known as {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Power|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and Shrine Power|qq=}}. As a result, Keidai Toshi functioned as political centers for these religious powers.
=== Cultural Function === Keidai Toshi were also hubs of cultural activity, as major temples and shrines served as places of learning with significant status in medieval Japan. These cities provided not only religious education but also practical knowledge in fields such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural function drove their development as major economic centers, beyond their political and religious significance.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi maintained high levels of industrial production, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and by skilled occupational groups tied to religious organizations. For example, historical records indicate that prominent temples produced weapons and military supplies<ref>Examples include orders for shields and arrows at {{ill|Negoro-ji|ja|根来寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Negoro-ji|qq=}} in 1360, requests for 500 rifles and warrior monks by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ieyasu|qq=}} at {{ill|Mount Koya|ja|高野山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Koya|qq=}} in 1584, and Toyotomi Hideyoshi's prohibition of weapon manufacturing by temples in 1585.</ref>.
In addition to industrial production, Keidai Toshi also served as important trade hubs due to the movement of worshippers and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Many Keidai Toshi also had military functions. In addition to their economic power and production of military supplies, temples and shrines were often designed as fortifications<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be the {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|比叡山延暦寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enryaku-ji|qq=}} on Mount Hiei, built in 1166.</ref>. They maintained significant military forces, including warrior monks ({{ill|Sohei|ja|僧兵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sohei|qq=}}) and shrine warriors ({{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}}), and were capable of conducting independent military operations.
This military power often allowed Keidai Toshi to function as independent {{ill|Asylums|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asylums|qq=}}, free from external authority.

revision.text:
'''Keidai Toshi''' (境内都市) is a historical concept in {{ill|Japanese history|ja|日本史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese history|qq=}}, referring to the characteristics of [[:en:City|urban areas]] during the [[:en:Middle Ages|medieval period of Japan]] (roughly 12th to 16th centuries). It describes clusters of urban settlements centered around religious institutions such as [[:en:Buddhist temple|temples]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]].
The term "Keidai Toshi" was proposed by Japanese historian {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}} and has since gained support among scholars. In his own work, Ito remarked, "Honestly, I don’t understand why I was the first to point this out."<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 91.</ref> He also expressed dissatisfaction with the term’s phrasing, stating, "The term doesn’t sound right," and hinted at the possibility of renaming it in the future.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 247.</ref>
== Overview ==
Before the concept of "Keidai Toshi" was introduced, settlements originating from religious institutions were regarded as {{ill|Monzen town|lt=Monzen town|lt=Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}} (urban areas centered on commerce for worshippers). It was assumed that their primary function was religious, and their economic role was subordinate. Modern perceptions of religion as isolated from economic activities further reinforced the notion that Monzen-machi did not hold significant economic importance.
The concept of "Keidai Toshi" seeks to revise this perspective, suggesting that large settlements originating from religious institutions should be recognized as urban areas with political, cultural, industrial, and military functions.
The "Keidai Toshi" varied in scale, but the larger ones are thought to have had populations exceeding 100,000.<ref>伊藤(2008), p. 92.</ref> In medieval Japan, cities with tens of thousands of inhabitants were considered "large cities," contrasting sharply with the image of small settlements suggested by the term "Monzen-machi."
== Functions of Keidai Toshi ==
=== Political Function === Keidai Toshi were urban areas established based on large religious organizations such as major temples and shrines. These religious institutions formed a significant political force, known as {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple and shrine powers|qq=}}, rivaling the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court (Japan)]] and {{ill|samurai|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|qq=}} ({{ill|shogunate|ja|幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shogunate|qq=}}). Consequently, Keidai Toshi served as political centers of these religious authorities.
=== Cultural Function === As religious organizations often served as centers of learning in medieval Japan, Keidai Toshi played significant roles as cultural hubs. These "centers of learning" extended beyond religious studies to include practical disciplines such as martial arts, medicine, civil engineering, and agriculture. This cultural aspect contributed to the economic significance and growth of Keidai Toshi.
=== Economic Function === Keidai Toshi possessed advanced industrial production capabilities, supported by practical knowledge concentrated in these cities and skilled artisan groups associated with religious organizations. For example, records show that in 1360, {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|ja|畠山義深|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatakeyama Yoshifusa|qq=}} urged [[:en:Negoro-ji|Negoro-ji]] to deliver shields and bows, and in 1584, [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] requested 500 muskets and warrior monks from [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]].<ref>Such activities demonstrate the role of prominent temples and shrines in the military-industrial complex of the time.</ref>
Moreover, Keidai Toshi functioned as key trade hubs, facilitated by the movement of pilgrims and other travelers.
=== Military Function === Keidai Toshi often held military significance. In addition to possessing strong military power based on economic strength and arms production, temples and shrines were sometimes designed and constructed as fortresses.<ref>The earliest medieval mountain fortress in Japan is said to be [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]], built in 1166.</ref> They housed large numbers of [[:en:Sōhei|Warrior monks]] and {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}}, enabling independent military operations.
This military capability often made Keidai Toshi autonomous {{ill|asylums (law)|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/asylums (law)|qq=}} free from external control.
== Notes == <references/>
== References == * {{ill|Masatoshi Ito|ja|伊藤正敏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masatoshi Ito|qq=}}, ''Jisha Seiryoku no Chūsei'' (寺社勢力の中世), Chikuma Shobō, 2008. ISBN 978-4-480-06435-6. == See Also == * {{ill|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and shrine powers|ja|寺社勢力|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple and shrine powers|qq=}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|ja|寺内町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaimachi|qq=}} * {{ill|Right of asylum|ja|アジール権|fr|Droit d'asile|ja|アジール|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Right of asylum|qq=}} * [[:en:Yamashina Mido|Yamashina Hongan-ji]] / [[:en:Ishiyama Hongan-ji|Ishiyama Hongan-ji]] * [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]] * {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}} / [[:en:Sōhei|Warrior monks]]
